title: He never saw Constans alone.

source.title: NPNF2-04. Athanasius: Select Works and Letters

verses.book: Apology to the Emperor. (Apologia Ad Constantium.)
Apology to the Emperor. (Apologia Ad Constantium.)
verses.text: But in truth I am ashamed even to have to defend myself against charges such as these, which I do not suppose that even the accuser himself would venture to make mention of in my presence.

verses.book: Apology to the Emperor. (Apologia Ad Constantium.)
verses.text: For he knows full well that he speaks untruly, and that I was never so mad, so reft of my senses, as even to be open to the suspicion of having conceived any such thing.

verses.book: Apology to the Emperor. (Apologia Ad Constantium.)
verses.text: So that had I been questioned by any other on this subject, I would not even have answered, lest, while I was making my defence, my hearers should for a time have suspended their judgment concerning me.

verses.book: Apology to the Emperor. (Apologia Ad Constantium.)
verses.text: But to your Piety I answer with a loud and clear voice, and stretching forth my hand, as I have learned from the Apostle, ‘I call God for a record upon my soul,’ and as it is written in the histories of the Kings (let me be allowed to say the same), ‘The Lord is witness, and His Anointed is witness,’ I have never spoken evil of your Piety before your brother Constans, the most religious Augustus of blessed memory.

verses.book: Apology to the Emperor. (Apologia Ad Constantium.)
verses.text: I did not exasperate him against you, as these have falsely accused me.

verses.book: Apology to the Emperor. (Apologia Ad Constantium.)
verses.text: But whenever in my interviews with him he has mentioned your Grace (and he did mention you at the time that Thalassus came to Pitybion, and I was staying at Aquileia), the Lord is witness, how I spoke of your Piety in terms which I would that God would reveal unto your soul, that you might condemn the falsehood of these my calumniators.

verses.book: Apology to the Emperor. (Apologia Ad Constantium.)
verses.text: Bear with me, most gracious Augustus, and freely grant me your indulgence while I speak of this matter.

verses.book: Apology to the Emperor. (Apologia Ad Constantium.)
verses.text: Your most Christian brother was not a man of so light a temper, nor was I a person of such a character, that we should communicate together on a subject like this, or that I should slander a brother to a brother, or speak evil of an emperor before an emperor.

verses.book: Apology to the Emperor. (Apologia Ad Constantium.)
verses.text: I am not so mad, Sire, nor have I forgotten that divine utterance which says, ‘Curse not the king, no, not in thy thought; and curse not the rich in thy bedchamber: for a bird of the air shall carry the voice, and that which hath wings shall tell the matter.’

verses.book: Apology to the Emperor. (Apologia Ad Constantium.)
verses.text: If then those things, which are spoken in secret against you that are kings, are not hidden, it is not incredible that I should have spoken against you in the presence of a king, and of so many bystanders?

verses.book: Apology to the Emperor. (Apologia Ad Constantium.)
verses.text: For I never saw your brother by myself, nor did he ever converse with me in private, but I was always introduced in company with the Bishop of the city where I happened to be, and with others that chanced to be there.

verses.book: Apology to the Emperor. (Apologia Ad Constantium.)
verses.text: We entered the presence together, and together we retired.

verses.book: Apology to the Emperor. (Apologia Ad Constantium.)
verses.text: Fortunatian, Bishop of Aquileia, can testify this, the father Hosius is able to say the same, as also are Crispinus, Bishop of Padua, Lucillus of a Verona, Dionysius of Lëis, and Vincentius of Campania.

verses.book: Apology to the Emperor. (Apologia Ad Constantium.)
verses.text: And although Maximinus of Treveri, and Protasius of Milan, are dead, yet Eugenius, who was Master of the Palace, can bear witness for me; for he stood before the veil, and heard what we requested of the Emperor, and what he vouchsafed to reply to us.
